items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Once again I’m plagued
by vague misgivings...
Shpak’s missing a tape recorder,
and the ambassador’s missing a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core comedic effect (mock-heroic high register applied to petty suspicion) is preserved well via “plagued by vague misgivings.” Crucially, the translation correctly avoids the wrong possessive reading (“Shpak has...”), and instead conveys loss (“missing”), matching the reference note. Rhythm and parallelism are kept. Minor point: adding “missing” twice slightly over-explicates compared to the punchier nominal juxtaposition in the reference, but it doesn’t harm the joke.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—very pleased.
Very pleased—hello, Tsar.

Tsar—very pleased.
Hello, Tsar—very pleased!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The intended humor—Bunsha’s era-clashing, repetitive Soviet-politeness loop while impersonating the Tsar—is largely preserved through the stilted repetition of “Tsar—very pleased.” However, the translation drops the self-introduction nuance (“I’m the Tsar”) that makes the greeting feel like a bureaucratic/formal introduction rather than simply addressing someone as “Tsar.” The repetition still reads funny, but the specific gag of repeatedly presenting himself as “the Tsar” is weakened.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and fully preserves the comic authoritarian register shift.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; keeps the archaic/high-register melancholy that drives the humor in a modern setting.

items.translation:
They’ve walled me in!
Walled me in, demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning and comic misunderstanding are preserved. The only slight loss is the ellipsis/dragged-out despair (“…”) that can heighten the melodramatic tone; exclamation marks make it more immediate/angry than lamenting, but still works.

items.translation: So that’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and clear; preserves the religious attribution of a “miracle.” Compared to the reference, “So that’s what…” is slightly more conversational/modern and less proclamatory (“Behold the power…”), which softens the mock-biblical flourish that helps the joke land in an atheist/Soviet context. Still effective, but less stylized.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is understandable and functions as a command to start dancing, but it loses the specific performative/MC-like formulaic feel of the Russian (“Now everyone dances!” as a showman’s announcement). “Everybody dance!” reads like broken/ESL English and is less witty than a naturalized announcement (“Everybody’s dancing!” / “Everyone dance!” / “All right—everybody dance!”). This is a noticeable fluency/style hit in a famous catchphrase moment.

items.translation: Well, this is my lucky day!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the upbeat self-satisfaction and opportunistic delight. “Lucky day” is a good idiomatic equivalent and keeps the comic tone. Slightly shifts from “I dropped in at the right time” to a broader “my day is lucky,” but the pragmatic function (gleeful appraisal of a profitable situation) remains intact.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
Exotic delicacy—eggplant spread!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Successfully avoids calling eggplant spread “caviar,” aligning with the reference warning. The contrast between luxury and banal spread is preserved. Minor loss: the comedic beat of the false build-up (“Yes! Overseas…”) is compressed; “Exotic delicacy—” approximates it but is less of a reveal. Still lands.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is all gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestically made cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The enumerative comic structure and the escalating “three” are preserved, including the final “a suede jacket—three,” which keeps the absurd inflation. “Domestically made” is a reasonable rendering of “отечественных.” Minor stylistic point: “movie cameras” is fine, though “film cameras” might better match the period feel; not a real problem. Overall effective.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And you’ll be cured too!

And I’ll be cured too!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Meaning is preserved, but the delivery changes: the reference emphasizes a calm, almost sing-song resignation with ellipses, turning a dark reality (psychiatric ward) into shared absurdity. The translation’s exclamation marks make it more emphatic/cheerleader-like and less deadpan, weakening the specific comedic subversion described in the reference analysis. Still understandable and funny-ish, but the tonal precision is off.

summary.interpretation: 2.09 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.42 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
